subject: ap-chemistry
title: Stoichiometry & Limiting Reagents
stimulus:
A student designs an experiment to study the reaction between NaHCO
and HC
$H_2O$
. The reaction is
represented by the equation above. The student places 2.24 g of NaHCO
in a flask and adds 60.0 mL of
0.875 M HC
$H_2O$
. The student observes the formation of bubbles and that the flask gets cooler as the reaction
proceeds.

parts.prompt:
Identify the reaction represented above as an acid-base reaction, precipitation reaction, or redox reaction.
Justify your answer.

scoring_points.description: calculating the number of moles of each reactant

required_elements: Valid justification based on chemical principles
alternatives.correct_example: 1 mol NaHCO3 / 84.0 g × 2.24 g NaHCO3 = 0.0267 mol NaHCO3; 0.875 mol HC2H3O2 / 1000 mL × 60.0 mL = 0.0525 mol HC2H3O2

common_traps: Stating answer without connecting to the underlying chemical principle

parts.prompt: Based on the information above, identify the limiting reactant. Justify your answer with calculations.

scoring_points.description: identifying the limiting reactant consistent with the calculations

required_elements: Valid justification based on chemical principles
alternatives.correct_example: The NaHCO3(s) and HC2H3O2(aq) react in a 1:1 ratio, so the limiting reactant is NaHCO3(s).

common_traps: Stating answer without connecting to the underlying chemical principle

parts.prompt:
The student observes that the bubbling is rapid at the beginning of the reaction and gradually slows as the
reaction continues. Explain this change in the reaction rate in terms of the collisions between reactant
particles.

scoring_points.description: correct explanation of the change in reaction rate in terms of collisions

required_elements: Valid justification based on chemical principles
alternatives.correct_example: As the reaction proceeds, the concentration of the reactants decreases, resulting in fewer collisions between reactant particles per unit time and a lower reaction rate.

common_traps: Stating answer without connecting to the underlying chemical principle

parts.prompt:
In thermodynamic terms, a reaction can be driven by enthalpy, entropy, or both.
(i) Considering that the flask gets cooler as the reaction proceeds, what drives the chemical reaction
between NaHCO
(s) and HC
$H_2O$(aq)? Answer by drawing a circle around one of the choices
below.
Enthalpy only Entropy only Both enthalpy and entropy
(ii) Justify your selection in part (d)(i) in terms of ΔG°.
−

The HCO
ion has three carbon-to-oxygen bonds. Two of the carbon-to-oxygen bonds have the same
length and the third carbon-to-oxygen bond is longer than the other two. The hydrogen atom is bonded to
−
one of the oxygen atoms. In the box below, draw a Lewis electron-dot diagram (or diagrams) for the HCO
ion that is (are) consistent with the given information.

A student prepares a solution containing equimolar amounts of HC
$H_2O$
and NaC
$H_2O$
. The pH of
the solution is measured to be 4.7. The student adds two drops of 3.0 M HNO
(aq) and stirs the sample,
observing that the pH remains at 4.7. Write a balanced, net-ionic equation for the reaction between
HNO
(aq) and the chemical species in the sample that is responsible for the pH remaining at 4.7.
M + I → MI
2 2

scoring_points.description: correct balanced net-ionic equation for the buffer reaction
